speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, you were saying what happened when the train stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we-- before we got out, my mother said, put on this coat, this long coat, and the hat, because at least even if that take away our suitcase, at least what you have got on you're allowed to keep. So then in winter, you'll have at least a coat and a hat to keep you warm. So we jumped out. And the first orders were men and women to separate. So that was really the last time I saw Heinz. We-- he helps me down the train first. And then we cuddled and hugged. And then he went with my father, and walked to the other side. And my father and my mother embraced. And my father embrace me too. And he said, God will protect you. I remember that. That was the last words he said, because before he had always said, he doesn't really believe in a God. But then he said God will protect you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to him, there is a God, huh? So he said, yeah, Eva, yeah, there is. So-- and then we saw them walking past. And then the next order was rows of five. Can I just ask you if you remember what was the last thing you said to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we just-- we just-- we were both crying. And we were just holding each other. And I don't think we could say anything. And so the next order was rows of five. So we went in rows of five. And next to us was an older lady, and a woman, and a baby. The mother held the baby. And so we went forward. We had to walk forward. And there, stood six, seven Nazis. Later, we realized it was Mengele who stood there with gloves, and polished boots, very, very spotlessly and clean looking-- nice looking, I would say. He was quite a youngish man. He was, perhaps, 40, very distinguished looking. And he looked everybody over. And [GERMAN], he chose the people in front of us. And then when our row came, he said to the mother, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that the baby-- the baby, give to that woman. She the baby-- the older woman took the baby. And the mother started to cry. I want to go with my baby. I want to go with my baby. And my mother said-- no-- and Mengele said, no, no, and, you know, he pushed. And then my mother-- and the woman cried. And my mother said, this was a very nice lady. She'll look after your baby. Don't worry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then the mother said, but if it's long, I won't even recognize my child. And my mother said, but the old lady will recognize you. So don't worry. Don't worry. So the woman settled down a little bit. But, anyway, all-- and then they called out all the people who are a bit tired of the journey, and don't want to walk, there are buses there. And you can ride with the buses into camp. So quite a few people who would have passed, really, into that camp walking went on the bus. And those were the people who went straight to the crematorium as well. And those were the people that the Germans thought, well, perhaps they are a bit lazy, or don't want to-- or, really, are tired. So that was already a natural selection, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, luckily, we didn't. We walked. We went on. And all those people who went to the other side-- well, they were-- we didn't know that at the time. They went straight to the gas chambers. And we walked for, perhaps, 10 minutes into the concentration camp, which was all big towers, with posts on top, with Nazis, again, with machine gun pointing down at us. And the whole camp was with barbed wire, which was electrified. And there was big gates. And we could see there are lots of those different camps, all the same, but all one next to the other. And inside, we saw thousands of people milling around. And so we walked through there into a big building, all low. And what struck me that it was so kind of a muddy ground-- hard, it was dry. But no-- not earth, and no grass, and no tree, and no nothing-- completely just grey earth, and those grey barracks, and nothing else, and the barbed wire. Very- very depressing already, before you knew what it was going to be. So we went into this big hut. And it was a very, very hot day. May can be quite hot in Central Europe. And so the first-- no, already at the railway line, we had to leave all-- everything we carried, just leave everything there. So my mother said, see, good that you have your hat and coat. And this was actually one thing which had saved my life, because I looked older with a hat and a long coat. And I was one of the youngest people to enter a camp. There was as well a mother with a daughter who was 14, 15. And she was thinly dressed, and looked a child. And she never got into the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother now still always says to me, I saved your life with this hat and coat at the time. So-- so when we entered, they said, undress. So first, we took our jacket off or whatever, but not our underwear.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, everything, completely naked. And the Nazis, and the SS was walking around. So, you know, we-- and just shouted, undress, undress. And so, eventually, we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to stand for hours and hours in rows naked, waiting to be registered. And a lot of people that they're thirsty. We want a drink. And, no, there's nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some people even fainted. And then the capos, who are the people-- they're prisoners as well. But they have been long time. And they were already a little bit in higher position, because the Germans didn't really do the direct supervision. They were just on the top. But the real work with the us was the capos. They were mainly Poles, because they had been in the camp already much longer than the Jewish transports.

speaker: interviewer
text: What religion were the Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kind of political prisoners, and there were some Jewish. But I don't think there were many Jews still at this time. There were homosexual Poles, and criminals. There were a lot of criminals, murderers, and thieves, everything. And-- and they were quite tough, really, because they shouted to us, and said, when you were sill having all the comfort at home, we were already here building the camp. And all our people died. And they were, perhaps, even worse than the Germans, some of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we ask-- and, for instance, this mother asks, where has my baby gone? So they said, ha, ha. So they pointed up. She said, what you mean? So they said, well, haven't you seen the crematorium burning? Well, all those people have been gassed. You'll never see them again.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- you know, they said it quite happily. You know, it was cruel. And-- but one or two were quite kind. And when somebody was nearly fainting, they said, don't faint. Don't fall down, because you'll be right away selected. Stand close to each other so that if you fall that you fall on somebody that the Germans wouldn't see that you faint.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the naked bodies kind of stuck together. And we were in shock. It was quite terrible, this first moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we had to go in front of a table, all naked, and had to register our name, our profession, religion, everything. And most people gave up professions which they didn't have. So my mother said, you are a seamstress.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at least you can do some work if they want to know what you can do. I mean, I had no idea. But that's what we did. And I don't know. My mother said something too. But I don't know anymore what she said. We all had professions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but all-- and exact birth, and date, and everything, quite long sheets. And that gave us hope, actually. We thought, well, surely, they won't do all this if they are going-- intend to kill us. And then we had to go with our sheets of information in front to be tattooed our number, because from then on, though we had a name, it was all filed away then, the name with the number. And that's why later, in a way, they could find information, because it was really all there. It was typical Germans. They kept records, really, of everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, A5272. The next day, we were all called back. Somebody had made a mistake, had jumped one number. So we had to all be re-tattooed our number, because it didn't follow up. You know, there was one number missing. But as it happened with me, only one number was wrong. But with a lot of people, they had to cross out the whole number, and put a new number there. And my mother went in front. And then she said to the capo who's behind-- or to her about me, she said, look, she's only a child. Don't spoil her by giving a big number. Make it soft and small. And, indeed, she did that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I have got quite a small number. My mother has it double, and much stronger, and all the other people. And then-- Do you remember what it was like, standing there being tattooed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the tattoo was just little pinpricks. You know, that that wasn't so-- I wasn't painful as such. But the degradation that we were-- and then our sheet of information was taken away. And from then on, we were just a number. We had no name anymore. We were just a number. And that-- you know, like cattle. Then our head-- our hair was shaved. And then, again, you know, a head is not smooth. It's quite bumpy. And if you have a shaved head, it looks very ugly. And my mother said, again, look she's a young girl. Leave a little bit of hair in the front. And I had an inch of hair in the front standing so I didn't look quite as awful as all the others, you know, naked, and no hair. It really looked awful. And then we went to the shower. By then, we knew already how a shower-- the [INAUDIBLE] had told us, the capos had told us they had been gassed, you know, they went to a shower home. And they got the soap and towel.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they weren't going to a shower room. Instead of water, gas came. So we were already kind of a bit scared. Was it really going to be a shower room? Or was it going to be gas?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had-- I had supports. I had sort of flat feet, you know, as a child. And my mother said, you must keep your supports, because, you know, it's very bad for you to walk without your support. So she said, hide them-- when we get the clothes, hide them under your arm. And I had, indeed, got into the shower room still with my supports. So They were metal plates under my arm. And then she said, put it there on the ledge when-- when you were having the shower. So I put those-- very obedient, I put those there on the ledge. And then we stood there. And the doors closed with our soap. And they looked up, and really water came. So that was, of course, a great relief. And then we were-- after a few minutes, when we were washed, the doors on the other side opened. And we were kind of herded out. And I forgot my supports. And my mother was quite cross with me. She said, now your left your supports inside. But I couldn't go back. And then we came to big tables with heaps of clothes, and then also a table with heaps of shoes. And we were just thrown a garment. It could be a winter dress, a summer dress, a jacket, a coat, whatever. And at the shoe bit, we got two shoes. It could be a high heeled shoe and a flat shoe, two right shoes, just anything. So I said to my mother, see I couldn't where my supports in those shoes anyway. And they had-- so that was-- then later, we swapped, what would fit better. But that was all we had, nothing else. And the capos had told us, like I told you, we were very thirsty. And we wanted water. And we didn't get any.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said, you'll go now to your barracks. And you'll wait till you get your evening meal. But don't-- whatever you do, never drink any water, because it's not clean. And you might get typhus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we passed a tap from somewhere on the barrack. And it dripped. There was a little water coming out. And I said to my mother, I just must drink. And I went there. And I drank a little bit. And then we walked on. And we were put into a barrack, which was-- it used to Birkenau. It was not Auschwitz. It was Birkenau, which was a military-- this whole Auschwitz-Birkenau was from the camp from the Polish army before the war. And Auschwitz camp, where were the officers lived. And Birkenau, those buildings, the barracks, were where the horses where. So there was nothing in it. Only in the middle of it, there was a big cement kind of wall going from one end to the other. And that was heated in winter for the horses. So that was like an oven. And it was hot. But for us, there was no heating. The horses were heated for the army. But we never had any heating in there. And then there were-- each block had about 500 people. And each camp had on both sides 10 barracks of those. So in each little camp, there were already thousands of people. And at the end of each of those 10 blocks, there was a toilet and washing facilities. And that was it. And we were allowed to go twice a day to the toilet as a group, or wash once a day. And those bunk beds, they were just kind of cemented holds about a yard wide and a yard high, and three on top of each other. And in each hole, 10 people were sleeping. So, I mean, we were all our Dutch, from our Dutch transport. So with people we had made a little friends, we chose one of those bunk cubbyholes. And then we got our sort of a liquid, and a ration of bread. And they said, this is what you'll get every evening, just a piece of bread, and sort of a tea. That will be your food. And every evening and every morning, you have to stand appell, which was a role call of the camp. All the camps had to be counted twice a day. And it takes about two hours. And you will stand behind your block, your barrack, again in rows of five so that it can be quickly counted. And as long as it takes-- if it rains, if it's freezing, whatever. That's what you do. So we got up at about 5:00 o'clock every morning, had to stand two hours appell. Then we had to go to our work commando, which varied. And when we came back in the evening, again two hours appell.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at that time, it was very hot. And with our shaved heads and-- you know, our ears got burned. And our head got burned. And it was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- and we were in-- this first block where we went was a quarantine block. As if there were not enough illnesses anyway. But three weeks, we were not going to work. We had to stay in this block. It had kind of a-- was barbed off again that we couldn't mix with the other inmates. So if we would bring any illnesses. I don't know what they would have done. But, anyway, that was their rule.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for three weeks, at least we didn't work. But, possibly, from having drank that water, I got after a couple of days, I got terrible diarrhea. And we were only allowed twice a day to go to this end block where the toilets were. So in the morning we went. But after a few hours, I had terrible cramp. And I asked, can I go to the toilet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, of course not, you can't go. So I said to him, I just have to go. I just can't keep it in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I ask again. Can I go? No. So I just went to a corner, and did it. And the capo caught me, and grabbed me, and hit me, and said, [GERMAN]. Of course you get illnesses. Of course you die if you do those things all over the place. How can you? You're like a dog.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you get punishment. And I had to come forward. And she gave me a little wooden stool. And I had to kneel for two hours. And all the people had to watch. And I remember how my mother looked. She couldn't help me. You know, she couldn't-- so she saw how I had to kneel with this stool on top off me, and you know, diarrhea, and everything. That was-- I mean, that was the beginning of my camp life. But those toilet facilities and washing facilities, that was as well a big barrack. And there were holds, 50 holds, on each side. And in the middle was a platform where a capo walked. And we had to sit on this-- sort of on this hole. And I remember my father saying, you must never sit on it. So I tried to kind of not to sit, and to stand. But this capo walked on there. And if she saw somebody was not sitting, she hit you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had no toilet paper, nothing, so. And the washing, that was another block where there were, again, a long row, with taps, and basins, and with a little bit of water. It just trickled. It wasn't much water. And 500 people wanted to go at this. So there was very limited washing facilities at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and for three weeks, we were in quarantine with just doing appell, getting our food, and not doing anything for three weeks. So we just talked what we were going to do, and how life was going to be, and nothing really happened. You know, long days were very long. And in a way, we longed to get out and do something. And one day, the capo came, and said, today, you all stand in a row. And you're going to be sorted out into work commandos. And so they-- the Germans did that. So the SS came, and walked in front of me. And I looked a bit better than most, because I had my hair. And I was one of the youngest.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said to me, Canada. So we knew by then what Canada was. That was a work commando where you worked to sort out the clothes, and the food, and whatever the transports brought.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we knew that was a good commando, because you found food. And you didn't have to work that much. And it was a better commando then-- so when I heard Canada, I was very pleased.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had the cheek. I wasn't long enough yet in camp. I ask him, the German, I said, can my mother come too? And he said-- he was quite perplexed that an inmate should talk to him. That was unheard of. And he said, who is she? And I said, this. And, you know, she was, perhaps, 38, something like that. And she was a very good looking woman. Though she was shaved, she still looked all right, because they went by if people were attractive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he said, all right. So we got in there, in this good working commando. Later, the capo, she said, you devil. How dare you speak to a German. But, you know, it just-- it just came out. I just said that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you at this point, you mentioned about the toilet facilities, and the lack of anything to clean yourself with afterwards. Was there an occasion when you ever found anything to wipe yourself with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One day when we were walking after-- some time we had a little bit of time went after appell, and we didn't go to bed yet. Usually, we went very early to bed, because we were so tired. But in the long summer evenings, we sometimes strolled around. And there was-- behind the building, we saw a big roll of something white. And as I said, oh, that's paper. That's paper. And I went there. And I took a bait. And then when-- next, I went to the toilet. I said, I can wipe my bottom now with paper. And I did it. And it was kind of a for building. It was all with bits of glass. It was kind of insulation material, covered in glass splinters.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you can imagine I was completely bleeding, and sore. And it was terrible. So that was my experience with my bit of toilet paper, I thought.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody ever-- that was another peculiar. Nobody ever got a period in the camp. But the minute you got in, you didn't. And they said there was some bromide, which was supposed to be in the bread and in the liquid. I don't know. That was what they told us. I don't know if it's true. And while we were in the work commando, we had one of-- a great miracle happened. One day, I got a boil on my neck. And it was really very, very painful, and awful. And there was sort of-- there was a hospital. But it wasn't really a hospital. It was where Dr. Mengele worked. And he did his experiments there. And people did-- were put in there. But practically nobody ever came out of it, because when you were ill, you know, you were really no use. And you were selected out there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So nobody really wanted to go into hospital. When I had this diarrhea and I got ill, all the people in my bunk said that you must go to hospital. And I said, no, I don't want to go, because then I won't come out. So they kind of kept me. But I was very, very ill. But with this boil, I said, I have to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was so out of a surgery. You could go if you wanted something. It all sounds reasonable. But it wasn't reasonable. It was all-- you didn't really get medicine. And you didn't really get care. But it was all there. And so my mother said, all right, we'll go one day to this surgery. And we went there. And when we were waiting, there was a nurse walking. And I saw my mother looking. And I saw this nurse looking. And, suddenly, they fell into each other's arms. And it was a cousin of my mother who had been deported from Prague and sent to Auschwitz. And her husband was a skin specialist. And when-- you know, when he filled in probably that he was a skin specialist, the German picked him out. And he became-- he had to work for the Germans for-- because there were a lot of skin illnesses. He had to work for the Germans. And he was in touch with Mini. Mini was the name of that [INAUDIBLE]. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Sameck, I don't know his first name. And-- and he was-- he knew as well Mengele. So-- well, with this Mini is a very, very interesting story, which I will tell you a little bit more in details later.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll stop here.
